mnemonics.title: McCabe's Complexity Formula
mnemonics.content: E N2 P: Edges minus Nodes plus 2 times Programs (E - N + 2P) - Just remember 'Elephants Never Forget: E - N + 2P'

mnemonics.title: Decision Points Method
mnemonics.content: D + 1: Decision points plus One - Simple way to calculate complexity without drawing CFG

mnemonics.title: Basis Path Purpose
mnemonics.content: BAPS: Basis path ensures Complete Branch coverage with Minimum test cases

acronyms.usage: Visual representation of program flow showing nodes (statements/decisions) and edges (execution paths)
Quantitative measure of program complexity, equals number of independent paths to test
Method to calculate complexity by counting regions in the flow graph

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a maze - each decision point (fork in the path) adds one more possible route through the maze. The complexity tells you how many different routes you must try to cover all possibilities.
Think of a subway map - stations are nodes (statements/decisions), and train tracks between stations are edges showing possible travel directions.
Each independent path is like a unique route through a city that visits at least one new street corner not seen on other routes.
Count the 'rooms' or 'areas' created when you draw the CFG on paper - each enclosed area plus the outside area equals the complexity.

analogies.analogy: It's like testing a video game by playing through all unique storylines. You don't need to replay every minor variation, just each distinct path that leads to different outcomes.
Think of complexity like the number of ingredients in a recipe. Simple sequence = 1 ingredient (complexity 1). Adding decisions (if/else) = adding ingredients, each making the recipe more complex.
Like exploring a building through different entrances - each entrance takes you through areas not seen from other entrances, covering the whole building efficiently.
Testing all light switches in a house - you flip each switch both ON and OFF to ensure all wiring works, covering all branches of electrical flow.
